repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem: Currently there is no way for `vim.pack` to load plugins from disk in a way that doesn't involve cloning.

It can be solved by putting plugins in a dedicated 'pack/*/opt' directory (either explicitly or via symlink) and loading them with `:packadd[!]`. But this splits plugin management into two ways, which for some people is very inconvenient.

Supporting this can be useful for several scenarios:
- Plugin is not a Git repo.
- Plugin is under a development by the user. Outside of symlinking, it can be installed via `file://...` source, but it means having to deal with two copies of the plugin. One special subcase of this is contributing to a managed plugin that is currently being used. Which typically involves creating and cloning a fork on Git forge. It usually is cloned in the directory with the same name as the upstream, so it should be possible to load a plugin with the directory name already present in 'pack/core/opt'.

Solution: Update `vim.pack.add()` to load plugins that point to an existing path on disk. In all cases it is assumed that plugin's management (i.e. install/update/delete) is done entirely outside of `vim.pack`, hence the naming of "unmanaged".

---

Resolve #34765

Notes:

- I am not particularly fond of adding this functionality to `vim.pack`. I still think that resolving such cases manually (by explicitly putting or symlinking to a dedicated 'pack/mine/opt' package) is a good enough solution. But I promised to try to resolve this with `vim.pack`, so this is the attempt.

- The term "unmanaged plugin" is the same as [used in 'junegunn/vim-plug'](https://github.com/junegunn/vim-plug/blob/60dd7a5eaf3ec98287e82bc6c2e7f99f31951c04/doc/plug.txt#L154). I think it is more directly shows the intention behind its support. Plus using "local" in Lua code is problematic.

    Plus I think it is more future friendly to have "local plugin" and "unmanaged plugin" be separate things. Maybe something like `version = false` in the future can be used to mark currently managed plugin as unmanaged. Although I personally don't really like the idea of unmanaged plugins in the first place.

comment_data.body:
```suggestion
      -- Plugins with local (non-URI) path are loaded but not managed.
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -259,6 +267,9 @@ Basic install and management ~
         -- Git branch, tag, or commit hash
         version = 'main',
       },
+
+      -- Plugins with local source are loaded but not managed

comment_regular_data.body:
> Solution: Update `vim.pack.add()` to load plugins that point to an existing path on disk.

@lewis6991 does this address your use-case?

To summarize, `vim.pack.add('/foo')` behaves as "loaded but not managed". So e.g. `pack.get()` won't return the plugin.

> I am not particularly fond of adding this functionality to `vim.pack`. I still think that resolving such cases manually (by explicitly putting or symlinking to a dedicated 'pack/mine/opt' package) is a good enough solution. But I promised to try to resolve this with `vim.pack`, so this is the attempt

Yeah. I don't really see much value in this, but if this PR satisfies #34765 , the cost looks ok. But I wonder what other requirements will pop up...

I think so.

My current manager also allows to update these plugin (by doing `git pull --fast-forward`), so I'l continue using that.

I noticed this will also make creating testcases a bit easier. Currently, you are required to use a custom `XDG_DATA_DIR` to create isolated testcases with `vim.pack`.

> I noticed this will also make creating testcases a bit easier. Currently, you are required to use a custom `XDG_DATA_DIR` to create isolated testcases with `vim.pack`.

If you mean tests for `vim.pack` itself, then it won't as those require managed plugins.

If you mean reproducible examples, then yeah maybe. But for those I'd still recommend using a clean `NVIM_APPNAME`.

> If you mean reproducible examples, then yeah maybe. But for those I'd still recommend using a clean NVIM_APPNAME.

... and `XDG_DATA_DIR` as that is where plugins will be downloaded to.

> > If you mean reproducible examples, then yeah maybe. But for those I'd still recommend using a clean NVIM_APPNAME.
> 
> ... and `XDG_DATA_DIR` as that is where plugins will be downloaded to.

No, `NVIM_APPNAME=nvim-test nvim` is enough. Plugins will be downloaded into '~/.local/share/nvim-test/site/pack/core/opt'.
You do not want that if a testcase requires patches, or general debugging. It's better to isolate all testcase data to a single dir, not somewhere buried in the users XDG dirs which is 7 levels deep.

Could we do the symlink _before_ potentially calling the user defined load function? Currently in this PR, this will not work, and will throw an error

```lua
vim.pack.add({
	{ src = "~/projects/plugins/roslyn.nvim" },
}, {
	load = function(plugin)
		vim.cmd.packadd(plugin.spec.name)
	end,
})
```

Something like this (probably not the best way to do it, but just something I threw together to show the intent), makes the above code not throw an error

```diff
diff --git a/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua b/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua
index f518a3db87..5f51f751a1 100644
--- a/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua
+++ b/runtime/lua/vim/pack.lua
@@ -788,6 +788,16 @@ local function infer_update_details(p)
   p.info.update_details = table.concat(newer_semver_tags, '\n')
 end
 
+local function try_unlink(plug, tmp_plug_path)
+  if not plug.info.managed then
+    -- Adjust 'runtimepath' to contain original path. Don't force using absolute
+    -- path as `:packadd` adds based on `stdpath('data')` (which can be relative).
+    local plug_path_pattern = vim.pesc(tmp_plug_path):gsub('/', '[\\/]')
+    vim.o.runtimepath = vim.o.runtimepath:gsub(plug_path_pattern, plug.path)
+    uv.fs_unlink(tmp_plug_path)
+  end
+end
+
 --- @param plug vim.pack.Plug
 --- @param load boolean|fun(plug_data: {spec: vim.pack.Spec, path: string})
 local function pack_add(plug, load)
@@ -804,18 +814,15 @@ local function pack_add(plug, load)
     unmanaged_plugins[plug.path] = true
   end
 
-  if vim.is_callable(load) then
-    load({ spec = vim.deepcopy(plug.spec), path = plug.path })
-    return
-  end
+  local plug_name = plug.spec.name and plug.spec.name
+    or ('_unmanaged_%d'):format(vim.tbl_count(unmanaged_plugins))
+  plug.spec.name = plug_name
 
-  local plug_name = plug.spec.name
   local tmp_plug_path = ''
   if not plug.info.managed then
     -- Temporarily symlink into main plugin directory to reuse `:packadd` and
     -- have entry order in 'runtimepath' as if it is a managed plugin
     -- Use (hopefully) unique name to avoid conflicts with existing plugins
-    plug_name = '_unmanaged_' .. vim.tbl_count(unmanaged_plugins)
     tmp_plug_path = vim.fs.joinpath(get_plug_dir(), plug_name)
 
     -- Ensure that linking uses absolute path to better work with junction links
@@ -824,16 +831,16 @@ local function pack_add(plug, load)
     uv.fs_symlink(plug.path, vim.fs.abspath(tmp_plug_path), link_opts)
   end
 
+  if vim.is_callable(load) then
+    load({ spec = vim.deepcopy(plug.spec), path = plug.path })
+    try_unlink(plug, tmp_plug_path)
+    return
+  end
+
   -- NOTE: The `:packadd` specifically seems to not handle spaces in dir name
   vim.cmd.packadd({ vim.fn.escape(plug_name, ' '), bang = not load, magic = { file = false } })
 
-  if not plug.info.managed then
-    -- Adjust 'runtimepath' to contain original path. Don't force using absolute
-    -- path as `:packadd` adds based on `stdpath('data')` (which can be relative).
-    local plug_path_pattern = vim.pesc(tmp_plug_path):gsub('/', '[\\/]')
-    vim.o.runtimepath = vim.o.runtimepath:gsub(plug_path_pattern, plug.path)
-    uv.fs_unlink(tmp_plug_path)
-  end
+  try_unlink(plug, tmp_plug_path)
 
   -- The `:packadd` only sources plain 'plugin/' files. Execute 'after/' scripts
   -- if not during startup (when they will be sourced later, even if
```

> Could we do the symlink _before_ potentially calling the user defined load function?

I don't think it is a good idea.

The symlink here is a technical hack to make default loading of unmanaged plugins possible with `:packadd`. This saves a lot of extra code and the result is aligned with how managed plugins are added (like order in 'runtimepath', for example). The symlink is not permanent and is removed right after `:packadd`.

A proper handling of plugins outside of `pack/*/opt` directories is to manually adjust 'runtimepath' and source all necessary scripts. It is doable, but going the `:packadd` route is simpler.

The custom `opts.load` is there specifically to allow full takeover over how loading is done. Performing `:packadd` based on a seemingly random directory (which is outside of `pack/*/opt`) is not a good idea in itself. Also note that symlink is done with some private (hopefully unique) name and exposing that to the `opts.load` is also not clean.

FWIW, coming from lazy which also allowed the behavior of just pointing to a local directory, so far this PR solves my use-case nicely. Hopefully some flavor of this can make it in.

(I also don't use git for my version control locally, so initially vim.pack didn't work for those plugins at all. Much less with the seamlessness of developing in an unmanaged directory like with this PR.)

This is the biggest (and almost *only*) pain-point I've had switching to `vim.pack`. While managing symlinks in `~/.local/share/nvim/site/pack/foo/opt/` is not the end of the world, I agree with echasnovski's and lewis' points.

That aside, is there anything technical blocking this PR right now? (Seems like the PR needs to be caught up to current master)

I'm still [looking for](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/37727#issuecomment-3853625595) a concrete problem this actually addresses. lewis even mentioned he doesn't plan to use this.

So what is actually solved by this? Give a concrete, micro-example that we can show in the docs.

Lazy lets you set a "dev" dir, in which plugins marked with `dev = true` get cloned to. After being cloned, they are not automatically updated, and the plugin is loaded from the current working tree

This means setting up a new install boils down *exclusively* to downloading your init.lua, your plugin projects end up where they're supposed to (if you're a heavy git user you might have a `[include gitDir:~/personal]` style setup), and switching between dev and regular is toggling 1 boolean + calling update.

As opposed to managing the entire thing by hand or writing a shell script to manage your dev plugins or reimplementing some subset of vim.pack yourself

```lua
vim.pack.add({ { src = "LunarLambda/alabaster.nvim", dev = true } }, { dev_dir = "~/git" })
```

that and the value add of being able to do lightning-fast reloads right from inside your git repo with ZR.

Basically the same on my side. I have a light adapter that takes a Lazy spec and does whatever is needed with `vim.pack`, autocmds, etc. This includes emulating a dev boolean like above, rewriting the source as needed.

This PR allows just pointing to a directory and things just work as I would have expected. It doesn't try to clone, and I can iterate on code changes to my plugins without any ceremony around it.

> While managing symlinks in `~/.local/share/nvim/site/pack/foo/opt/` is not the end of the world, I agree with echasnovski's and lewis' points.

*My* point is that using custom plugin package for "local plugins" is a good approach to solving this type of problem. This PR is only a result of my promising to try to solve this issue for people who vehemently refuse to do a one-time setup of such custom plugin package.

If it is a must have for a user to manage all project in a specific directory (like `~/Projects`), then doing a one-time symlink to `~/.local/share/nvim/site/pack/mine/{opt,start}` seems fine.

---

If I may, I'll link to [part of my blog post](https://echasnovski.com/blog/2026-03-13-a-guide-to-vim-pack.html#install-and-load) that describes in more details how I see a "good enough" managing of "local plugins" and contributing to already existing ones.

> If it is a must have for a user to manage all project in a specific directory (like `~/Projects`), then doing a one-time symlink to `~/.local/share/nvim/site/pack/mine/{opt,start}` seems fine.

~If I understand this correctly - this would require me to _undo_ this symlink and _redo_ it if I want to toggle between a local clone of a plugin or a github release version. This is something which I very frequently do, temporarily toggle a plugin between a local clone and a cloned version.~

**Edit**: I see in your post you demonstrate the ability to just swap out the pack.add with `vim.cmd.packadd('my-local-copy')` - which I suppose is fine, but it's still much worse than supporting local filepaths natively in vim.pack IMO. The reduction in cognitive overhead seem worth it?

I don't want the mental overhead of figuring out how to setup my symlinks correctly AND switching from `vim.pack` to `vim.cmd.packadd` - a different API - when my focus should be on developing some feature or fixing some bug. Lazy makes this easy for me because I can just go into my config and change the `https://...` to a `~/code/...`. Intuitive and 0 cognitive load.

> f I understand this correctly - this would require me to _undo_ this symlink and _redo_ it if I want to toggle between a local clone of a plugin or a github release version. This is something which I very frequently do, temporarily toggle a plugin between a local clone and a cloned version.

Please, see the linked blog post. Here is the quote from it describing the relevant step of setting up a "local" plugin:

> Download it (usually by forking it on a Git forge and using git clone) into a directory for “local” plugins. Use a directory name that is different from the reference plugin (otherwise there would be name conflicts).

So if there is `https://github.com/neovim/nvim-lspconfig`, setting up its "local" version can be done by cloning it directly as with `~/.local/share/nvim/site/pack/mine/opt/nvim-lspconfig-local`. Then:
- To toggle from a URL to a local: 1) comment out (full or only relevant part) of `vim.pack.add` that adds from URL; 2) add `vim.cmd.packadd('nvim-lspconfig-local')` call in the 'init.lua'.
- To toggle back - reverse the steps.

> I don't want the mental overhead of figuring out how to setup all my symlinks correctly, when my focus is on developing some feature or fixing some bug.

It can be done once.

> The reduction in cognitive overhead seem worth it?

I don't think it is in the grand scheme of things. My view is that the percentage of users that require this functionality is far smaller compared to users that use `vim.pack` to install plugins and use it as part of their editor. On the other hand, this PR adds non-trivial amount of complexity to code.

@echasnovski Yea sorry I read the post and edited my comment accordingly.

---

> I don't think it is in the grand scheme of things. My view is that the percentage of users that require this functionality is far smaller compared to users that use `vim.pack` to install plugins and use it as part of their editor. On the other hand, this PR adds non-trivial amount of complexity to code.

I would argue that while the people who want this feature are probably a smaller percentage of overall users - they are still a very important user-base as they represent the people developing and maintaining plugins used by the wider community.

Reducing the overhead for development and maintenance of plugins, and making it a first-class API, is arguably just as important for stimulating a healthy plugin community here.

Also, adding this feature does not detract in any meaningful way from the usability of this feature from the wider community.

---

Anyway that's just my 2$ I don't want make a big deal out of this. I think the workaround as you propose it is fine, I just think it would be valuable to have this functionality as well.

Let see what the consensus ends up being :)

I think just doing what Lazy does with dev = true would be simple and exactly what I want (clone once on install to a dir set by me, skip update/delete). That way plugins are still tied to the git mechanism, and local paths don't need to be special cased at all. I might make my own PR for this.

> I think just doing what Lazy does with dev = true would be simple and exactly what I want (clone once on install to a dir set by me, skip update/delete). That way plugins are still tied to the git mechanism, and local paths don't need to be special cased at all. I might make my own PR for this.

This provides *much* less leverage as it doesn't add the ability to load plugins that do not use Git. And requires a whole new field in the plugin specification.

The only difference with this PR is the automated vs manual installation.

---

> I would argue that while the people who want this feature are probably a smaller percentage of overall users - they are still a very important user-base as they represent the people developing and maintaining plugins used by the wider community.
> 
> Reducing the overhead for development and maintenance of plugins, and making it a first-class API, is arguably just as important for stimulating a healthy plugin community here.

@julienvincent, I am one of that small percentage and comfortable plugin development is still possible with current `vim.pack` functionality. As described in the "Well, technically..." part of the blog post. It is collapsed by default as I don't feel like it should be too public, since its usage requires being comfortable with fixing broken config. Which is expected from plugin maintainers.

> This provides much less leverage as it doesn't add the ability to load plugins that do not use Git. And requires a whole new field in the plugin specification

It works slightly differently than was presented in that comment. `dev = true` AIUI works by checking a configured directory for a path matching the name of the given plugin (derived usually by repo name), and if it matches, loads the plugin from that directory instead of installing it. Updates are skipped, and deleting the plugin from your config won't delete the directory.

The operation is not tied to a specific plugin fetching mechanism.

I've implemented ~80% of this in my config by monkeypatching `vim.pack.add`. Currently, I do manually manage symlinks, but it could be made automatic with a little elbow grease. 

(I am perfectly happy with my solution and have no dog in this race… but wanted to clarify that point)

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: feat(pack)!: support unmanaged plugins
